explanation: The statement "The controller shall implement the following modes" implies that the controller must always be operating in exactly one of the specified modes. This translates into two LTL conditions that must hold globally (G): first, at least one of the listed modes must be active (exhaustivity), and second, no more than one mode can be active at any given time (mutual exclusivity). The exhaustivity is captured by the disjunction of all propositions. The mutual exclusivity is captured by the conjunction of the negations of all possible pairwise conjunctions of propositions.

The statement "The controller shall implement the following modes: prop_0, prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, prop_5" implies that at any given time, the controller must be in exactly one of these specified modes. This translates into two fundamental conditions that must hold globally (G):
1.  **At least one mode is active:** One of the listed propositions must be true. This is represented by the disjunction: `(prop_0 | prop_1 | prop_2 | prop_3 | prop_4 | prop_5)`.
2.  **At most one mode is active (mutual exclusivity):** No two modes can be simultaneously active. This is ensured by negating the conjunction of every possible pair of modes. For example, `!(prop_0 & prop_1)` means `prop_0` and `prop_1` cannot both be true at the same time. This applies to all unique pairs.

The LTL formula combines these two conditions with a global operator (G) to signify that this exact-one-mode state must be maintained throughout the system's operation.

This translates to two conditions that must hold globally (G): first, at least one of the modes must be active (represented by the disjunction of all modes); second, at most one mode can be active, meaning that no two modes can be simultaneously true (represented by the conjunction of negations of all possible pairs of modes).

The statement "The controller shall implement the following modes: prop_0, prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, prop_5" implies that at any given time, exactly one of these propositions must be true. This can be broken down into two conditions: 1. At least one mode is active: This is represented by the disjunction of all specified modes. 2. At most one mode is active: This means no two distinct modes can be active simultaneously, represented by the conjunction of the negations of all possible pairs of modes. These two conditions must hold simultaneously at all times, which is captured by the G (Globally) operator.

This translates to two conditions: (1) at least one of the modes must be true, and (2) at most one of the modes can be true (i.e., they are mutually exclusive). Both conditions must hold globally.

This is expressed by combining two conditions under the Global (G) operator: first, at least one of the propositions must be true (exhaustive), and second, no two distinct propositions can be true simultaneously (mutual exclusion).

The phrase 'implement the following modes' implies that at any given time, exactly one of the listed modes must be active. This means two conditions must hold globally (G): (1) at least one of the modes (prop_0, prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, prop_5) must be true, and (2) at most one of them can be true (i.e., no two modes are simultaneously active).

The phrase "shall implement the following modes" implies that the controller must always be in exactly one of the listed modes. This means two things: 1. At least one of the modes must always be active. 2. No more than one mode can be active at any given time (they are mutually exclusive). The 'G' (globally) operator is used to ensure this condition holds at all times. The first disjunction (prop_0 | ... | prop_5) ensures 'at least one'. The series of implications (prop_i -> (!prop_j & !prop_k & ...)) ensures 'at most one' by stating that if a specific mode is active, all other modes must be inactive.

The natural language statement implies that the controller must always be in exactly one of the specified modes. This requires two conditions to hold globally: 1. Exhaustivity: At least one mode must always be active. This is represented by the disjunction of all specified propositions. 2. Exclusivity: At most one mode can be active at any given time. This is represented by negating the conjunction of every possible pair of propositions. The final LTL formula combines these two conditions with a global operator (G).
